items.subtopic: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)
items.title: Evolution of Disease Nosology
items.content: Disease nosology transitioned from holistic descriptions to clinicopathologic observations in the late nineteenth century, focusing on end-organ manifestations and pathoanatomy. Morgagni's work exemplified this shift by correlating clinical features with autopsy findings.
keyPoints: Modern disease nosology emerged in the late 19th century.
Diseases were characterized by clinicopathologic observations.
Morgagni correlated clinical features with over 600 autopsies.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)
items.title: Limitations of Reductionist Disease Classification
items.content: Reductionist approaches in disease classification, while uncovering many mechanisms, often fail to account for the molecular complexity and variability seen in diseases. This is evident in conditions like sickle cell disease, where a single mutation does not predict all clinical manifestations.
keyPoints: Reductionism has limitations due to molecular complexity.
Single mutations do not explain all disease manifestations (e.g., sickle cell disease).
Biologic variety and genetic complexity underpin disease diversity.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)
items.title: Shift Towards Precision Medicine
items.content: The limitations of reductionist approaches have led to a shift towards precision medicine, which integrates genomic context for more precise and individualized disease definitions. This approach acknowledges the roles of epigenetics and environmental influences.
keyPoints: Precision medicine integrates genomic context into disease definition.
Acknowledges the role of epigenetics and environmental factors.
Aims for more precise and individualized disease understanding.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.title: Limitations of Traditional Disease Classification
items.content: Traditional disease classification simplifies diagnosis and therapy but has significant predictive inaccuracies, with up to 60% of patients not responding to the most effective drugs.
keyPoints: Predictive inaccuracies in traditional disease classification can be substantial.
Up to 60% of patients may not respond to the most 'effective' drugs due to these limitations.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.title: Role of Genomic Variation in Precision Medicine
items.content: Genomic variation, whether inherited or acquired, refines diagnostic precision and predictive accuracy, aiding the development of personalized treatment strategies.
keyPoints: Genomic variation enhances diagnostic precision and predictive accuracy.
Understanding genomic context is crucial for predicting disease manifestation.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.title: Convergent and Divergent Phenotypes in Disease
items.content: Patients with different diseases can exhibit similar pathophenotypes, while those with the same disease may show varied manifestations due to genomic context and unique exposures.
keyPoints: Convergent phenotypes occur when different diseases manifest similarly.
Divergent phenotypes arise when the same disease shows varied manifestations.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.title: When is Precision Necessary in Precision Medicine?
items.content: In precision medicine, the level of genomic and environmental detail required should be actionable. Excessive testing without actionable outcomes can lead to unnecessary anxiety and risks.
keyPoints: Precision is necessary when it leads to actionable outcomes.
Excessive precision can result in unnecessary testing and associated anxiety and risks.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 3)

items.title: Overdetermined Molecular Networks in Precision Medicine
items.content: The molecular networks governing phenotype are often redundant, allowing precision medicine to be effective without complete genomic knowledge. Primary drivers of disease expression can be identified and modified by other genomic features.
keyPoints: Molecular networks are overdetermined (redundant).
Primary drivers of disease expression can be modified by other genomic features.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 3)

items.title: Importance of Deep and Orthogonal Phenotyping
items.content: Deep phenotyping involves a comprehensive assessment including detailed history, functional studies, imaging, and molecular tests to refine disease phenotypes. Orthogonal phenotyping assesses seemingly unrelated clinical or molecular features to enhance subphenotype distinction.
keyPoints: Phenotyping includes family history, environmental exposures, physiological studies, and molecular imaging.
Endophenotyping refines discriminant power by assessing biochemical, immunologic, and molecular tests.
Orthogonal phenotyping enhances ability to distinguish subphenotypes by evaluating unrelated clinical or molecular features.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.title: Role of Genomic Profiling in Precision Medicine
items.content: Genomic profiling, including DNA sequencing and gene expression analysis, must be integrated with detailed phenotyping to understand disease mechanisms. Emerging areas like immunophenotyping are gaining importance for disease indication.
keyPoints: Genomic assessment includes exome, whole genome sequencing, mRNA/protein expression, and metabolomics.
Epigenome, proteome modifications, and metagenome (personal microbiome) are emerging genomic elements.
Immunophenotyping uses the immune system as a disease indicator and sensing system.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.title: Challenges in Correlating Genomic Data with Disease Phenotypes
items.content: Correlating genomic data from blood with organ-specific diseases is challenging due to tissue specificity. Biomarker signatures guide diagnostics but often show weak correlations, necessitating careful analysis before clinical application.
keyPoints: Gene expression and metabolomics are often tissue-specific, complicating correlation with whole-blood profiles.
Systemic consequences of organ-specific diseases can provide prognostic information.
Weak correlations between blood markers and organ-based diseases require condition-specific analysis.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.title: Importance of Timed Sampling in Genomic Profiling
items.content: Timed sampling is crucial for precision genotyping and phenotyping as gene expression patterns and phenotypes change over time. The optimal strategy for acquiring timed samples depends on cost, sampling frequency feasibility, predictive power, and therapeutic choices.
keyPoints: Gene expression patterns and phenotypes evolve differently among patients with the same overarching phenotype.
Cost reduction in genomics technologies may mitigate current limitations.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.title: Challenges of Genomic Profiling in Cancer
items.content: Cancer genomic profiling is frequently performed to monitor somatic mutations and oncogenic pathways. However, challenges include high mutation frequency, unknown functional consequences of many mutations, variability in single-cell mRNA sequencing, and interactions between stromal and malignant cells.
keyPoints: Frequent sampling of cancers helps monitor temporal changes in the somatically mutating oncogenome.
Functional interpretation is challenged by great variability in single-cell mRNA sequencing patterns.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.title: Identifying Therapeutic Targets through Precision Medicine
items.content: Precision medicine aims to identify therapeutic targets beyond simple DNA sequencing by analyzing gene expression and constructing protein-protein interaction networks. This approach helps link genotype to phenotype, enabling rational drug targeting or repurposing.
keyPoints: Protein-protein interaction network (interactome) maps are used to create individualized 'reticulomes'.
Example: PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway activation in multicentric Castleman's disease led to sirolimus trials.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.title: Precision Medicine in Heart Failure: Tailoring Therapy to Pathophysiologic Phenotypes
items.content: In heart failure, precision medicine involves tailoring treatment based on specific pathophysiological phenotypes such as congestion, hypertension, and impaired contractility. This approach has evolved from the use of a combination of therapeutic agents like diuretics, beta blockers, and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone inhibitors to more refined subclassifications including heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and preserved ejection fraction.
keyPoints: Therapeutic classes include diuretics, digoxin, beta blockers, afterload-reducing agents, venodilators, renin-angiotensin-aldosterone inhibitors, and brain natriuretic peptide (nesiritide).
Heart failure is subclassified into heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and preserved ejection fraction.
Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction does not respond well to most current therapeutic agents.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Genomic Characterization in Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension
items.content: Precision medicine has transformed the treatment of pulmonary arterial hypertension by identifying distinct genotypes that correlate with unique phenotypes. This includes recognizing a fibrotic endophenotype associated with NEDD-9 and its role in collagen III expression, leading to more targeted therapies.
keyPoints: Therapies include calcium channel blockers, prostacyclin congeners, and endothelin receptor antagonists.
Recognition of distinct genotypes such as a fibrotic endophenotype governed by NEDD-9.
Targeted therapies for specific phenotypes like perivascular fibrosis.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Precision Medicine in Cancer: Targeting Oncogenic Pathways
items.content: Cancer treatment has been revolutionized by precision medicine through the identification of a limited number of oncogenic pathways (<20) across malignancies. This has led to targeted therapies such as imatinib for Bcr-Abl tyrosine kinase in chronic myeloid leukemia and erlotinib for EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung cancers.
keyPoints: Limited number of oncogenic pathways (<20) identified across malignancies.
Targeted therapies include imatinib for Bcr-Abl tyrosine kinase, erlotinib for EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung cancers, and ibrutinib for Bruton tyrosine kinase in chronic lymphocytic leukemia.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Challenges of Precision Therapeutics in Cancer
items.content: Precision therapeutics for cancer face unique challenges due to the evolving mutational landscape, limited curative potential of single agents, genomic heterogeneity within tumors, and patient variability. These factors necessitate rational polypharmaceutical approaches and immune modulation strategies.
keyPoints: The mutational landscape evolves as cancer progresses, leading to resistant clones.
Cancer is unlikely to be cured by any single agent due to the need for targeting alternative pathways.
Genomic heterogeneity within tumors complicates long-term success of targeted therapies.
Patient variability affects treatment tolerance and immune response.
Combination therapies with immune modulators offer promise despite these challenges.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Precision Medicine in Infectious Diseases
items.content: Infectious disease treatment parallels cancer precision medicine by identifying causative organisms and their antimicrobial sensitivities. Combinatorial treatments address resistance, while individual responses may vary due to different endophenotypes, suggesting potential for improved outcomes through precise mechanistic understanding.
keyPoints: Identification of causative organisms allows precision treatment in infectious diseases.
Combinatorial antimicrobial treatments help manage acquired resistance.
Individual variability in response to infections and treatments is influenced by different inflammatory responses or endophenotypes.
Immune modulation, especially for chronic infections, represents a new frontier in personalized medicine.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 2)

items.title: The Role of Data in Precision Medicine
items.content: Precision medicine relies on comprehensive phenotypic and genomic data for effective analysis. The integration of large datasets, including time trajectories, is crucial for refining precision medicine practices.
keyPoints: Deeply refined personal phenotypic data and genomic data are essential for precision analysis.
Large datasets require sufficient storage and advanced analytical methods to extract useful information.
Machine learning and artificial intelligence are critical for optimizing data extraction.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · THE FUTURE OF PRECISION MEDICINE

items.title: Expanding Phenotyping in Precision Medicine
items.content: Phenotyping must become more comprehensive, incorporating diverse data types such as personal device data and environmental exposure history to enhance disease trajectory prediction.
keyPoints: Phenotypic features should include clinical presentation and orthogonal data for better insights.
Data harmonization and standardization are necessary for effective phenotyping.
Incorporation of various data sources is key to defining phenotype.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · THE FUTURE OF PRECISION MEDICINE

items.title: Determining Minimal Data Sets in Precision Medicine
items.content: Identifying the minimal necessary data set for predicting outcomes and therapy responses remains a significant challenge, requiring advanced analytical strategies to optimize data use.
keyPoints: The challenge lies in analyzing data to eliminate redundancy and accurately weight outcome determinants.
Machine learning and AI are essential for handling complex biological systems.
Predictive enrichment uses big data-driven analyses to define cohorts likely to respond to therapies.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · THE FUTURE OF PRECISION MEDICINE

items.subtopic: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)
items.question: Which publication by Morgagni correlated clinical features with autopsy findings, demonstrating an anatomical basis for disease pathophysiology?
options: De Sedibus et Causis Morborum per Anatomen Indagatis
items.explanation: Morgagni's publication 'De Sedibus et Causis Morborum per Anatomen Indagatis' correlated clinical features with autopsy findings.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)
items.question: What is a significant shortcoming of the Oslerian diagnostic approach mentioned in the passage?
options: It fails to distinguish underlying etiologies with common pathophenotypes.
It relies too heavily on genetic testing.
It focuses excessively on environmental factors.
items.explanation: The Oslerian diagnostic approach suffers from the inability to distinguish underlying etiologies of diseases that have common pathophenotypes.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)
items.question: What did the Human Genome Project promise in terms of disease understanding?
options: To identify a monogenic, oligogenic, or polygenic cause for every disease.
To eliminate all genetic diseases through gene editing.
To provide complete cures for all known diseases.
To map out environmental influences on all diseases.
items.explanation: The Human Genome Project promised to identify a monogenic, oligogenic, or polygenic cause for every disease.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)
items.question: Why did the reductionist approach in molecular medicine fail to fully explain clinical manifestations of diseases?
options: It assumed a single molecular mechanism could explain all disease manifestations.
It focused too much on holistic approaches.
items.explanation: The reductionist approach failed because it assumed that a single molecular mechanism could explain all clinical manifestations of diseases.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.question: What has been a natural outgrowth of the observational scientific method in disease nosology?
items.explanation: Oversimplification of phenotype, which involves categorizing individuals into similar groups or clusters.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.question: What is a limitation of the traditional approach to disease diagnosis and therapy?
options: It lacks efficiency in large populations
It results in significant predictive inaccuracies
It does not use standard diagnostic tests
items.explanation: The traditional approach has limitations including significant predictive inaccuracies and segments of the disease population that do not respond to effective drugs.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.question: What drives new classifications of diseases like heart failure with preserved versus reduced ejection fraction?
options: New technologies or applications of established technologies
items.explanation: Differences in pathogenotype leading to new classifications are often driven by new technologies or ways of applying existing ones.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.question: What does modern genomics establish about genomic context?
options: It is identical for all individuals
It provides insight into disease manifestation based on individual differences
It eliminates the need for personalized medicine
items.explanation: Modern genomics establishes that genomic context, or modifier genes, is distinctive for each person and helps predict how a primary disease driver may manifest clinically.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.question: In precision medicine, why is complete knowledge of all genomic dimensions not necessary?
options: Because the molecular networks governing phenotype are overdetermined and have primary drivers.
Due to the high cost associated with comprehensive genomic testing.
Since environmental factors play a more significant role than genetic factors in disease expression.
Owing to the lack of technology to analyze all dimensions of the genome.
items.explanation: Precision medicine can be effective without complete genomic knowledge because molecular networks are redundant and have primary drivers that influence disease expression.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 3)

items.question: What is a potential consequence of pursuing excessive precision in medical testing?
options: Improved patient outcomes due to detailed data.
Increased anxiety and risks associated with unnecessary testing.
Reduced healthcare costs through targeted interventions.
Enhanced understanding of environmental influences on health.
items.explanation: Excessive precision in medical testing can lead to increased anxiety and risks without providing actionable benefits.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 3)

items.question: What is the primary consideration for determining the level of precision needed in precision medicine?
options: The ability to acquire complete genomic data.
Whether the additional precision leads to actionable outcomes.
The patient's preference for detailed information.
items.explanation: More precision is needed in precision medicine when it results in actionable outcomes; otherwise, it may lead to unnecessary testing and anxiety.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically included in deep phenotyping for precision medicine?
options: Detailed history including family and environmental exposures
items.explanation: Deep phenotyping involves detailed history, functional studies, imaging, and biochemical tests but not genomic profiling.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.question: What is the purpose of orthogonal phenotyping in precision medicine?
options: To evaluate unrelated endophenotypes for enhanced subphenotype distinction
To focus solely on genomic profiling
items.explanation: Orthogonal phenotyping aims to enhance the ability to distinguish subphenotypes by assessing seemingly unrelated clinical, molecular, or functional endophenotypes.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.question: Which genomic feature is often tissue-specific and may require correlating whole-blood profiles with those of the involved organ?
items.explanation: Gene expression, metabolomics, and epigenomics are often tissue-specific, necessitating correlation with whole-blood profiles.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.question: What is a key determinant of the functional consequences of a genetic variant in disease phenotypes?
options: Coexpression of protein binding partners in specific pathways
Presence of the variant in blood tests
Conversion to induced pluripotent stem cells
items.explanation: The coexpression of protein binding partners in specific (dys)functional pathways is crucial for understanding the functional consequences of a genetic variant.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.question: Which factor is NOT mentioned as driving the optimal strategy for acquiring timed samples in precision genotyping and phenotyping?
items.explanation: The passage mentions cost, feasible sampling frequency, predictive power, and therapeutic choices as factors driving the optimal strategy.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.question: What is a unique limitation of cancer genomic profiling mentioned in the passage?
options: High frequency of somatic mutations and unknown functional consequences
Lack of temporal changes in oncogenome
items.explanation: The passage states that the frequency of somatic mutations is high, especially with treatment, and many mutations have unknown functional consequences.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.question: What does the construction of a protein-protein interaction network help achieve in precision medicine?
options: Identification of single-cell mRNA sequencing patterns
Linking genotype to phenotype through disease modules
items.explanation: The passage explains that constructing a protein-protein interaction network helps link the genotype to the phenotype by mapping disease modules.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.question: In precision medicine, what is an example of drug repurposing mentioned in the passage?
options: Use of sirolimus for multicentric Castleman's disease
Application of azathioprine for TPMT variants
Employment of a new drug for PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway
items.explanation: The passage mentions the repurposing of sirolimus, an existing drug approved for other purposes, for multicentric Castleman's disease due to activation of the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following therapeutic agents is NOT mentioned as being commonly used in heart failure treatment?
items.explanation: ACE inhibitors are not listed among the common agents for heart failure; instead, renin-angiotensin-aldosterone inhibitors and other classes are mentioned.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 1)

items.question: In the context of pulmonary arterial hypertension, what was a significant advancement in treatment during the postgenomic era?
options: Discovery of distinct genotypes yielding unique phenotypes
items.explanation: The recognition of distinct genotypes that yield unique phenotypes, such as the fibrotic endophenotype governed by NEDD-9, was a significant advancement.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary reason neuromyelitis optica can now be classified as separate from multiple sclerosis?
options: Discovery of autoantibodies against AQP-4 and MOG
items.explanation: The discovery of autoantibodies against AQP-4 and MOG allowed neuromyelitis optica to be classified as a separate entity.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 1)

items.question: In the pregenomic era, how was chemotherapy for cancers primarily guided?
options: According to tissue affected and histologic characteristics
items.explanation: Chemotherapy in the pregenomic era was based upon the tissue affected and histologic characteristics of the tumors.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the primary challenges associated with precision therapeutics in cancer?
options: The mutational landscape evolves as the disease progresses.
A single agent can definitively cure any cancer.
Genomic heterogeneity complicates long-term targeting of specific pathways.
Variability exists in patient characteristics and their ability to withstand treatment.
items.explanation: The passage states that the likelihood of curing any cancer with a single agent is quite limited, not definitive.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 2)

items.question: What strategy in infectious disease treatment mirrors approaches used in precision medicine for cancer?
options: Identification of causative organisms and their antimicrobial sensitivity.
Randomized drug trials without prior organism identification.
Development of vaccines for all known pathogens.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that precision approaches in infectious diseases involve identifying the causative organism and its sensitivity to potential antimicrobials.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 2)

items.question: What is a common strategy used in both cancer and chronic infections to improve treatment outcomes?
options: Immune modulation, particularly for immune exhaustion.
Targeting only the primary oncogenic pathway.
items.explanation: The passage highlights that immune modulation is a strategy used in both cancer and chronic infections to improve treatment outcomes.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which data types are essential for precision medicine analysis according to the passage?
items.explanation: Both deeply refined personal phenotypic data and genomic data are essential for precision analysis.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · THE FUTURE OF PRECISION MEDICINE

items.question: What is required to ensure appropriate comparison data sets in precision medicine?
options: Data on the 'normal' segments of the population
Only data from individuals with diseases
items.explanation: Gathering sufficient information on the 'normal' segments of the population is required for appropriate comparison.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · THE FUTURE OF PRECISION MEDICINE

items.question: What will be essential for extracting optimal information from large data sets in precision medicine?
options: Machine learning and artificial intelligence methods
items.explanation: Machine learning and artificial (auxiliary) intelligence methods are essential for extracting optimal information from large data sets.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · THE FUTURE OF PRECISION MEDICINE

items.question: What is a major challenge in making precision medicine the standard approach to illness?
options: Determining the minimal data set required to predict outcome and response to therapy
items.explanation: A major challenge is determining the minimal data set required to predict outcome and response to therapy.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · THE FUTURE OF PRECISION MEDICINE

items.subtopic: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)
items.statement: The era of molecular medicine began in the late nineteenth century.
items.explanation: The era of molecular medicine began in the mid-twentieth century.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)
items.statement: Epigenetics plays a significant role in disease manifestation, influenced by environmental exposures.
items.explanation: The passage explains that epigenetics is significantly modulated by environmental exposures and individual experiences.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.statement: Genomic variation can refine diagnostic precision with greater fidelity.
items.explanation: For many diseases, genomic variation provides opportunities to improve diagnostic precision and predictive accuracy.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.statement: Patients with the same disease always manifest similar pathophenotypes.
items.explanation: Patients with the same basic disease can manifest very different pathophenotypes due to genomic context and unique exposures.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.statement: Complete knowledge of a person's comprehensive genome is necessary for effective precision medicine.
items.explanation: Effective precision medicine does not require complete genomic knowledge due to the redundancy in molecular networks and primary drivers of disease.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 3)

items.statement: Precision medicine can be realized without acquiring quantitative assessments of environmental and social history.
items.explanation: Despite the challenges in acquiring complete data, precision medicine is feasible due to the overdetermined nature of molecular networks.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 3)

items.statement: Most genomic features are currently available for clinical laboratory testing.
items.explanation: Many genomic features, such as the epigenome and metagenome, are not yet available for clinical laboratory testing.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.statement: Orthogonal phenotyping assesses endophenotypes unrelated to clinical presentation.
items.explanation: Orthogonal phenotyping involves assessing seemingly unrelated clinical, molecular, or functional endophenotypes.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.statement: The passage suggests that genomic features in chronic diseases are typically assessed at multiple time points.
items.explanation: The passage states that genomic features tend to be limited to single time point sampling.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.statement: Assessment of gene expression patterns in cancer is straightforward due to minimal variability.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that there is great variability between apparently similar cells, challenging functional interpretation.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.statement: Precision medicine approaches have led to a new classification of dementias based on genes, pathways, and protein aggregation sites.
items.explanation: Dementias are now categorized by the involved genes and pathways and where proteins first aggregate in the nervous system.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: In heart failure, all therapeutic agents respond equally well to both reduced ejection fraction and preserved ejection fraction phenotypes.
items.explanation: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction does not respond well to most of the current therapeutic classes.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Precision medicine approaches can cure all cancers with a single agent.
items.explanation: The passage states that the likelihood of curing any cancer with a single agent is quite limited.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in infectious diseases can be applied without detailed knowledge of personalized responses.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that these strategies can be applied with good outcomes in most cases, aside from serious adverse effects.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Machine learning methods are not necessary for analyzing large precision medicine data sets.
items.explanation: Machine learning and artificial intelligence methods are essential for extracting optimal information from these data sets.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · THE FUTURE OF PRECISION MEDICINE

items.statement: Phenotyping must expand to include only clinical presentation data.
items.explanation: Phenotyping must incorporate not only clinical presentation data but also orthogonal phenotypic data.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · THE FUTURE OF PRECISION MEDICINE

items.subtopic: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)
items.assertion: The Human Genome Project led to the identification of a monogenic cause for every disease.
items.reason: The project provided new tools and approaches to identify genetic causes for diseases.
items.explanation: While the Human Genome Project aimed at identifying genetic causes, it did not lead to the identification of a monogenic cause for every disease.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)
items.assertion: Clinicopathologic observation has been foundational in defining diseases.
items.reason: It allowed for the correlation of clinical signs with pathoanatomy through autopsy material.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why clinicopathologic observation was foundational.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · Precision Medicine and Clinical Care | DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.assertion: Precision medicine requires understanding the interplay among complex molecular determinants of disease expression.
items.reason: This is essential for success in precision medicine as it involves genomic context and unique exposures over an individual's lifetime.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why understanding these determinants is crucial.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.assertion: Traditional diagnostic approaches have completely eliminated predictive inaccuracies.
items.reason: Targeting larger groups of patients with standard therapies has been highly efficient.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as traditional approaches still have significant predictive inaccuracies; the reason is true but does not support the incorrect assertion.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · DISEASE NOSOLOGY AND PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.assertion: Immunophenotyping can be used as a sensing system for new diseases.
items.reason: The immune system acts as an indicator of disease or prior exposures.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.assertion: Single-cell gene expression data simplify understanding disease phenotypes.
items.reason: These data provide spatial distribution information for different cell types.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; single-cell gene expression adds complexity, not simplicity.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 1)

items.assertion: Precision medicine aims to identify specific therapeutic targets.
items.reason: This requires analysis beyond simple DNA sequencing, including gene expression and functional consequences.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why precision medicine involves more than just DNA sequencing.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.assertion: The interactome provides a comprehensive map of protein interactions in an organ.
items.reason: This mapping helps identify potential drug targets by linking genotype to phenotype.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how the interactome aids in identifying drug targets.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · REQUIREMENTS FOR PRECISION MEDICINE (part 2)

items.assertion: In precision medicine, genomic signatures have led to targeted therapies for cancers.
items.reason: There are a limited number of oncogenic pathways represented in the majority of malignancies.
items.explanation: The limited number of oncogenic pathways has allowed for precisely targeted therapies based on genomic signatures.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Pregenomic precision medicine was ineffective in treating pulmonary arterial hypertension.
items.reason: There were no effective therapies for this condition prior to the 1990s.
items.explanation: While there were no effective therapies before the 1990s, pregenomic approaches did include treatments like calcium channel blockers.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · EXAMPLES OF PRECISION MEDICINE APPLICATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Precision medicine requires the integration of personal device data and environmental exposure history.
items.reason: These elements are part of defining phenotype in precision medicine.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · THE FUTURE OF PRECISION MEDICINE

items.assertion: The boundaries to the practice of precision medicine are strictly precise.
items.reason: Precision medicine uses detailed big data-driven analysis for predictive enrichment.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; while features can be collated or clustered, the boundaries are not strictly precise.
items.reference: Precision Medicine and Clinical Care · THE FUTURE OF PRECISION MEDICINE
